summary: O'Neil presents three examples of algorithmic discrimination to counter claims that data science is objective. The cases include a black woman who received dramatically more job offers after creating a white persona, predictive crime algorithms that use physical characteristics as proxies for race, and a British university's admissions algorithm that amplified existing gender and racial biases. She frames these as predictable failures that vindicate her earlier warnings about big data's supposed neutrality. The post directly challenges Kenneth Neil Cukier's claims about data science objectivity, suggesting these problems will only multiply.
key_points: Mandatory diversity questionnaires on job sites can enable rather than prevent discrimination, as demonstrated by the dramatic difference in callbacks between black and white identities
Predictive algorithms that use physical characteristics like hair, eye, and skin color serve as proxies for race, reinforcing discriminatory practices
Algorithms don't just reflect existing biases—they can magnify them, as shown by a university admissions system that exacerbated discrimination
These failures are not surprising edge cases but predictable outcomes that critics had warned about
Claims of data science 'objectivity' collapse when confronted with real-world discriminatory outcomes
money_quotes: I'd love to hear Kenneth Neil Cukier explain how 'objective' data science is when confronted with this stuff.
So I decided to try an experiment: I created a fake job applicant and called her Bianca White.
The use of physical characteristics such as hair, eye and skin color to predict future crimes would raise 'giant red privacy flags' since they are a proxy for race.
What began as a time-saving exercise ended in disgrace when a computer model set up to streamline its admissions process exposed – and then exacerbated – gender and racial discrimination.
This is just the beginning, unfortunately.
key_insight: Big data systems don't eliminate human bias—they systematize and amplify it in predictable ways that defenders of algorithmic objectivity refuse to acknowledge.
